---
title: "Running FEMA analysis in DEAP"
subtitle: "Model specification, execution, and visualization"
date: last-modified
author: CMIG Tools
toc: true
number-sections: true
---

## Introduction {#sec-intro}

**FEMA** in DEAP fits **linear mixed effects models** to imaging outcomes???typically **voxelwise** or **vertexwise** maps. You specify **dependent variables** (imaging maps), **fixed effects** (predictors and covariates), optional **genetic principal components**, **interactions**, and **random effects** (for example family and subject). After the job completes, you open results in DEAP???s **surface** and/or **volumetric** viewers and explore statistical **layers** (for example betas, standard errors, and signed log *p*-values).

::: {.callout-note}
## Nuisance vs variables of interest
FEMA only **saves and emphasizes results** for fixed effects you mark as **variables of interest**. Covariates you want in the model but do not need full inferential output for should be left as **not** of interest.
:::

## Before you start {#sec-before}

- **Dataset:** You need a dataset that includes the imaging and covariate variables required for your model. Variable availability depends on the **study** and **release**; check the relevant **release notes** and data dictionary for your cohort.
- **DAIRC imaging inclusion:** For structural MRI and related imaging, you can choose whether to **include or exclude** scans that did not meet **DAIRC** inclusion criteria (as offered in the dependent-variable step). Use this control consistently with your analysis goals and reporting.

# Model specification

## Step 1 ??? Selecting a dataset {#sec-step1}

There are two common ways to select the dataset for an analysis.

1. **Analyze an existing dataset** from the DEAP **Home** page: choose **Analyze an existing dataset**, select the dataset, then proceed (for example via **Use selected dataset** / **Analyze dataset**, depending on the current UI).
2. **My Datasets:** Open your saved datasets, select one, and choose the action to **analyze** or **use** that dataset for analysis.

Follow the on-screen flow until you reach the analysis configuration where you specify the **model** and **tool**.

## Step 2 ??? Specify the model {#sec-step2}

### 2a ??? Select the FEMA tool {#sec-2a}

1. Open the **Analysis details** (or equivalent) section from the sidebar.
2. Choose **FEMA** as the engine to run a **linear mixed effects model** on your imaging outcomes.

### 2b ??? Specify the dependent variable(s) {#sec-2b}

1. Add one or more **dependent variables**. For many workflows, **Imaging map** is the primary type: it covers most **voxelwise** and **vertexwise** concatenated imaging data. (Additional modalities???for example resting-state fMRI correlation matrices or tabulated-only outcomes???may appear over time; consult the current DEAP release notes.)
2. Variables are usually grouped in a nested order: **MRI modality** (for example structural MRI, diffusion MRI) ??? **measure** (for example cortical thickness, fractional anisotropy) ??? **data type** (voxelwise vs vertexwise).
3. You may select **multiple** dependent variables. For **vertexwise** data, you often specify **smoothing** level; choosing multiple smoothing options typically creates **separate** dependent variables, one per option.
4. After adding variables, review the list of selections.
5. **Transformations on Y:** You can apply a transformation to **all** dependent variables in the set (according to the options presented).
6. **DAIRC inclusion:** Decide whether to **restrict** to scans meeting DAIRC inclusion criteria or to **include** scans that did not, using the control provided for your imaging type.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Example workflow
For a cortical thickness analysis, select **cortical thickness** under the appropriate modality, choose vertexwise options (including smoothing if needed), then confirm transforms and DAIRC handling.
:::

### 2c ??? Specify the fixed effect(s) {#sec-2c}

1. In the **Fixed effects** section, add predictors and covariates (**Add new** or equivalent).
2. **Variable source:** You can pull variables from **your dataset** and/or from **DAIRC-recommended** lists for imaging and non-imaging variables. If you use DAIRC-recommended variables, you do **not** need to have manually added every corresponding column to the dataset beforehand.
3. Add covariates from the presented lists (**Covariates**). You can add variables one at a time or use **Select all** where available, then **Add**.
4. The interface shows a scrollable list of all fixed effects you added.
5. **Reference / transform** options:
   - **Categorical variables:** Set the **reference** level. Defaults often include **Mode**; other presets can include **First**, **Last**, or **Custom**. **Custom** opens a dialog to pick the reference category explicitly.
   - **Continuous variables:** Choose a **transformation** (for example winsorization or splines). Splines and winsorization may ask for extra parameters; if you leave parameters blank, **defaults** apply.
   - **Global transform:** You may apply one transformation to **all** continuous fixed effects. If you set both a global transform and a variable-specific transform, the product documentation describes the **order** of application (typically variable-specific first, then global???follow the on-screen help for your release).
6. **Variables of interest:** Mark which fixed effects are **of interest** for full FEMA output. Effects that are **nuisance only** should remain **not** of interest.

::: {.callout-important}
## Variables of interest
Only effects marked **of interest** receive the full set of saved / emphasized inferential outputs in FEMA. Plan which effects you will interpret in manuscripts or tables before finalizing the model.
:::

### 2d ??? Genetic principal components {#sec-2d}

You can include up to **32** genetic principal components as fixed effects. These PCs **do not** need to be pre-added to your dataset; DEAP can attach them according to the study???s genetic PC definitions for your analysis context.

### 2e ??? Interaction terms {#sec-2e}

1. Add **interactions** among fixed effects you already specified (**Add new** in the interactions section).
2. The default is often a **two-way** interaction; use **Add variable** (or similar) to build **higher-order** (*n*-way) interactions.
3. Mark whether each interaction term is a **variable of interest** when prompted.

### 2f ??? Random effect(s) {#sec-2f}

By default, FEMA includes **Family** (**F**) and **Subject** (**S**) as random effects when appropriate; you can include or exclude them according to your design. **Additive genetic** (**A**) relatedness may be available depending on configuration and data. The **error** (**E**) term is **always** present.

::: {.callout-note}
## Features in development
**Mediation**, **custom contrasts**, and **stratification** variables may appear as ???coming soon??? or similar in the portal. Do not rely on this guide for those features until they are documented in the official DEAP release notes.
:::

### 2g ??? Advanced settings {#sec-2g}

Open **Advanced settings** (often a sidebar or panel in the upper area of the model screen) for additional fitting options for the linear mixed model (for example precision, binning, permutation-related choices???exact labels depend on release). Adjust these only when you understand their effect on estimation; defaults are appropriate for many analyses.

## Step 3 ??? Start the analysis {#sec-step3}

Submit or start the analysis from the DEAP workflow (for example **Run**, **Submit**, or **Start analysis**, depending on the UI). The job runs in the DEAP compute environment; wait until it completes before opening the viewers.

# Visualizing results {#sec-viz}

Navigate to **My analyses** (or equivalent) in the sidebar, locate your completed job, and use the **brain** or **viewer** action to open results.

## Surface viewer ??? Steps 4???6 {#sec-surface}

### Step 4 ??? Select the analysis to view {#sec-step4}

Same navigation as above: **My analyses** ??? select your analysis ??? open the **surface** / brain viewer.

### Step 5 ??? Select statistical layers {#sec-step5}

Choose which **fixed** and **random** effect layers to display, or load **all** layers associated with **My analysis** (wording may vary). Use **Expand layers** (or similar) to inspect individual layers you selected.

### Step 6 ??? Customize the surface viewer {#sec-step6}

**6a ??? Toggle layers:** Turn layers on or off to control what is drawn on the mesh.

**6b ??? Hemisphere and view:** The default may show **both** hemispheres. You can show **one** hemisphere, adjust **view** settings from the sidebar, and pick **presets** from the viewer toolbar.

**6c ??? Grouping:** Layers may be grouped **by variable** (betas, standard errors, test statistics, signed log *p*-values together per variable) or **by statistic type** across variables (for example all *T* statistics or all signed log *p*-values together).

**6d ??? Mosaic and linking:** The **mosaic** layout (often a four-panel icon) shows multiple views. **Linked** rotation applies the same rotation to panels; turning linking off rotates panels **independently**.

**6e ??? Vertex values:** **Double-click** a location on the cortical surface to show **numeric values** for the active layer in a **Results details** panel. Use **Global settings** (or the adjacent control) to return from detail view to display options.

## Voxel viewer ??? Steps 7???9 {#sec-voxel}

### Step 7???8 ??? Analysis and layers {#sec-voxel-layers}

Selecting the analysis and choosing **statistical layers** works analogously to the surface viewer (Steps 4???5).

### Step 9 ??? Customize the volumetric viewer {#sec-voxel-custom}

**Differences from the surface viewer:**

- There is **no mosaic** layout in the volumetric viewer.
- **Parcellation overlays:** Below fixed and random effect layers, you can add **parcellation** overlays. Three probabilistic atlases are commonly available (names may be prefixed by release, for example):

  - **Fiber / white matter tracts** (AtlasTrack-style tract labels).
  - **Subcortical-only** regions (FreeSurfer **aseg**-style).
  - **Subcortical and cortical** regions (FreeSurfer **aparc+aseg**-style).

**9a ??? ROIs:** Toggle individual ROIs on or off. Click the **color** swatch next to an ROI name to change its color. These are **probabilistic** maps: use the **threshold** control (often in the right sidebar). For example, a threshold of **0.75** means that in about **75%** of scans in the reference construction, that voxel was assigned to that ROI.

**9b ??? Anatomical plane:** To change the main viewing plane (for example **axial** vs **coronal**), use the **Pinned** (or equivalent) control for the main anatomical view.

# FAQ and troubleshooting {#sec-faq}

| Issue | Suggestion |
|-------|------------|
| A variable is missing from pick lists | Confirm it exists in your dataset and meets DEAP???s type / completeness rules; check release notes for renames. |
| Long runtime | Large samples, many vertices/voxels, or many permutations increase time; check job status and queue messaging in DEAP. |
| Too many layers | Use **Expand layers** and turn off unused layers; group by statistic to compare across predictors. |
| Interpreting maps | Compare **beta** maps to **standard error** and **signed log *p*** (or *Z* / *T* layers as labeled) consistently with your pre-registered hypotheses. |

For pipeline-level and JSON details, see the [Technical appendix](technical-appendix.qmd).
